---
title: "Text Analysis"
subtitle: Day 14
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: ../img/horst_stringr.jpeg
  data-background-size: 70%
  data-background-position: bottom right
  data-background-opacity: "0.8"
  data-slide-number: none
format: 
  revealjs:
    incremental: true
    code-copy: true
    code-link: true
    scrollable: true
auto-stretch: false
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)

library(countdown)
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(palmerpenguins)
library(patchwork)
library(ggthemes)
library(nycflights23)
library(here)
slides_theme = theme_minimal(
  base_family = "Atkinson Hyperlegible",
  base_size = 16)

theme_set(slides_theme)
```

## Today

1. Finish Regex
2. Intro to Text Analysis

# Regular Expressions (regex) {.maize}

## Regular expressions

Last time, we discussed

`str_view()` - finds regex matches

Specifications:

`.` - match any character

`[]` - match any occurence

`[^]` match any occurence except

and more! Including special patterns, anchors, and quantifiers

## Special patterns 

There are a number of special patterns that match more than one character

::: nonincremental 
- `\\d` - digit
- `\\s` - white space
- `\\w` - word
- `\\t` - tab
- `\\n` - newline
:::

::: aside
Outside of R, these would be single back slashes.
:::

## Special patterns

```{r }
a1 <- "Home: 507-645-5489"
a2 <- "Cell: 219.917.9871"
a3 <- "My work phone is 507-202-2332"
a4 <- "I don't have a phone"
info <- c(a1, a2, a3, a4)
str_view(a1, "\\d")
```

## Caution!

```{r }
str_view(a1, "\\w")
```

## Anchors

Anchors look for matches at the start `^` or end `$`

```{r}
#| echo: false
str_view(info)
```

::: panel-tabset

### Entries that start with a digit

```{r}
str_view(info, "^\\d")
```


### Entries that end with a digit

```{r}
str_view(info, "\\d$")
```

:::

## Your turn

::: task
A vector called `words` is loaded with `stringr` and contains a corpus of 980 words used in text analysis. Use `stringr` functions and regular expressions to find the words that satisfy the following descriptions. 

#### 1. Find all words that start with `y`.

```{r}
#| eval: false
pattern <- "type your pattern here"
str_subset(words, pattern)
```

#### 2. Find all words that end with `x`.

#### 3. Find all words that start with a vowel.

#### 4. Find all words that start with consonants.
:::

```{r}
#| echo: false

countdown::countdown(4)
```

## Alternative patterns

`|` allows you to match one or more alternative patterns

We want full state abbreviations that start with either A or C

```{r}
str_view(state.abb, "^A|C.")
```


## "Fixing" the order of operations

Why are these different?

::::: columns
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
```{r}
str_view(state.abb, "^A|C.")
```
:::


::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
```{r}
str_view(state.abb, "^(A|C).")
```
:::
:::::

## Quantifiers

You can search for a pattern that appears a certain number of times in a row

::::: columns
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
- `?`: 0 or 1
- `+`: 1 or more
- `*`: 0 or more
- `{n}`: exactly n times
- `{n,}`: n or more times
- `{,m}`: at most m times
- `{n,m}`: between n and m times
:::

::: {.column .nonincremental .fragment width="50%"}
Example: detect three digits in a row

```{r}
str_view(a1, "\\d{3}")
```

:::
:::::

## Your turn: 

::: {.task .nonincremental}
Go to regex101.com. Type your regex under "Regular Expression," your string under "Test String," and use the "Quick Reference" section as a cheat sheet if needed.

We are going to test whether two names, Alexander and Aaron, have two a's

- First, test whether each has an a. Also test whether each has an A. Notice the difference in what is highlighted under Test String. How could you test for both a and A at the same time? You should get two matches for both names under "Match Information."
- Now, test whether each name has two a's - make sure your test takes into account both lower and upper case a's. You should get a single match for both names under Match Information.
:::

```{r}
#| echo: false

countdown::countdown(5)
```

## Takeaways

Regex plays well with our stringr functions

regex101.com can help you troubleshoot your regex patterns if you're getting something unexpected in R

# Intro to Text Analysis {.maize}

## Text Analysis

Up to this point, we've been thinking of string data as a column in our dataset: 

  - Names
  - Phone numbers
  - Addresses
  - Course titles
  - etc. 

. . . 

which are all relatively short and structured. We can extract meaning from them using relatively simple functions and "know what we're looking for".


## Sometimes, text data is more unstructured

Random (?) sample of 26,882 reviews of coursera courses

```{r}
en_coursera_reviews <- read_csv("https://stat220kurtz.github.io/data/en_coursera_sample.csv")
en_coursera_reviews
```

::: aside
Source: [Kaggle](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/septa97/100k-courseras-course-reviews-dataset/data)
:::

*Note:* may need to install `tidytext` and `stopwords` packages

## Sometimes, text data is more unstructured

```{r}
en_coursera_reviews %>%
  filter(str_detect(Review, "ggplot")) %>%
  pull(Review)
``` 

## {.center}

::::: columns
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}

![](https://juliasilge.github.io/tidytext/reference/figures/tidytext.png){width="60%"}
:::

:::{.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
- Functions for text analysis that follow tidy data principles

- *Not* part of the `tidyverse`, but plays well with other packages

```{r}
library(tidytext)
```

:::
:::::

## Some notation

*document*: an observation of text data (in this case, a review)

. . . 

*corpus*: collection of many documents

. . . 

*token*: a unit of analysis within the document (typically a word, but could be a sentence or a line or a pair of words, etc.)

## Text as tidy data

1. *tokenize* each document
    - Each *row* in the dataset is a token
2. Remove *stopwords*
    - Words like "the" and "is" typically aren't interesting
    - Makes the dataset smaller and easier to work with
3. Count the tokens
4. (optional) *fancy count* the tokens
    - Standardize based on total number of tokens
    - How common is a token *relative to other documents*
    - How common are "good words" or "bad words"

## `unnest_tokens()`

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
- `tbl`: our data
- `output`: what should the output column be called?
- `input`: what column do we tokenize?
- `token`: default is "word" but can also be "ngrams", "sentences", "lines", "paragraphs", etc.
- `to_lower`: should tokens be converted to lowercase
:::
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
unnest_tokens(
  tbl,
  output,
  input,
  token = "words",
  to_lower = TRUE,
  drop = TRUE,
  ...
)
```
:::
:::::


## Tokenize

```{r}
library(tidytext)
en_coursera_reviews |>
  unnest_tokens(output = word, input = Review) |>
  select(CourseId, word)
```

## Stopwords

```{r}
get_stopwords(source = "stopwords-iso") |> pull(word)
```

## `anti_join`

![](../img/anti-join.gif)

## Remove stopwords

```{r}
#| code-line-numbers: "3"

en_coursera_reviews |>
  unnest_tokens(output = word, input = Review) |>
  anti_join(get_stopwords(source = "stopwords-iso"), by = "word") |>
  select(CourseId, word)
```

## Filter to include only data science courses

```{r}
#| code-line-numbers: "4"

en_coursera_reviews |>
  unnest_tokens(output = word, input = Review) |>
  anti_join(get_stopwords(source = "stopwords-iso"), by = "word") |>
  filter(str_detect(CourseId, "data-science")) |> 
  select(CourseId, word)
```

## Count the tokens, show most common by course id

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
#| code-line-numbers: "5-6|7|8-10"

en_coursera_reviews |>
  unnest_tokens(output = word, input = Review) |>
  anti_join(get_stopwords(source = "stopwords-iso"), by = "word") |>
  filter(str_detect(CourseId, "data-science")) |> 
  group_by(CourseId) %>%
  count(word) %>%
  slice_max(n, n = 10) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = fct_reorder(word, n))) + 
  geom_col() + 
  facet_wrap(~CourseId, scales = "free_y", nrow = 2) + 
  labs(
    y = "Word"
  ) + 
  theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 8))
```

## Make it ~ fancy ~: n-grams

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
- `token` is set to "ngrams"
- also specify `n=2` (2 words)
:::
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
unnest_tokens(
  tbl,
  output,
  input,
  token = "ngrams",
  n=2,
  to_lower = TRUE,
  drop = TRUE,
  ...
)
```
:::
:::::

## Make it ~ fancy ~: n-grams

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
#| code-line-numbers: "2,4,7"

en_coursera_reviews |>
  unnest_tokens(output = word, input = Review, token = "ngrams", n=2) |>
  anti_join(get_stopwords(source = "stopwords-iso"), by = "word") |>
  filter(str_detect(CourseId, "data-science")) |> 
  group_by(CourseId) %>%
  count(word) %>%
  slice_max(n, n = 10) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = fct_reorder(word, n))) + 
  geom_col() + 
  facet_wrap(~CourseId, scales = "free_y", nrow = 2) + 
  labs(
    y = "Word"
  ) + 
  theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 8))

```

## Removing stopwords from reviews *first*

```{r}
#| code-line-numbers: "2|3|5"
coursera_reviews <- en_coursera_reviews |>
  unnest_tokens(word, Review) |>
  anti_join(get_stopwords(source = "stopwords-iso"), by = "word") |>
  group_by(CourseId, review_id) |>
  summarize(review_clean = paste(word, collapse = " "))
```

. . . 

Original Review:

```{r}
en_coursera_reviews |> filter(review_id == 6) |> pull(Review)
```

. . . 

"Clean" Review

```{r}
coursera_reviews |> filter(review_id == 6) |> pull(review_clean)
```

## What if we use a different stopwords dictionary?

```{r}
coursera_reviews2 <- en_coursera_reviews |>
  unnest_tokens(word, Review) |>
  anti_join(get_stopwords(source = "snowball"), by = "word") |>
  group_by(CourseId, review_id) |>
  summarize(review_clean = paste(word, collapse = " "))
```

Original Review:

```{r}
en_coursera_reviews |> filter(review_id == 6) |> pull(Review)
```

Clean Review:

```{r}
coursera_reviews2 |> filter(review_id == 6) |> pull(review_clean)
```

## tokens on clean reviews

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
#| code-line-numbers: "3|4"

en_coursera_reviews |>
  left_join(coursera_reviews, by = c("CourseId", "review_id")) |>
  filter(str_detect(CourseId, "data-science")) |> 
  unnest_tokens(word, review_clean, token = "words") |>
  group_by(CourseId) %>%
  count(word) %>%
  slice_max(n, n = 10) %>%
  filter( n > 1) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = fct_reorder(word, n))) + 
  geom_col() + 
  facet_wrap(~CourseId, scales = "free_y", nrow = 2) + 
  labs(y = "word")
```

## Make it ~ fancy ~: tf-idf

Idea: how common are certain tokens *relative to the rest of the documents in the corpus*? 

. . . 

tf(t,d) =  "term frequency": what proportion of document d is term t? 

. . . 

idf = "inverse document frequency": log-scaled inverse fraction of documents that contain the term


$$\text{idf} = \log \frac{\text{number of documents in corpus D}}{\text{number of documents containing term t}}$$

. . . 

$$\text{tf-df(t, d)} = \text{tf(t, d)} \times \text{idf(t, D)}$$


## highest tf-idf's are used a lot within a review, and rarely in other reviews

```{r}
en_coursera_reviews |>
  unnest_tokens(word, Review) |>
  count(review_id, word) |>
  bind_tf_idf(word, review_id, n) |>
  group_by(word) |>
  mutate(total_n = sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  arrange(desc(tf_idf)) 
```

## 

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
#| code-line-numbers: "4|5-6|7,10"
en_coursera_reviews |>
  unnest_tokens(word, Review) |>
  count(CourseId, review_id, word) |>
  bind_tf_idf(word, review_id, n)  |>
  filter(str_detect(CourseId, "data-science")) |>
  group_by(CourseId) %>%
  slice_max(tf_idf, n = 10) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = fct_reorder(word, n))) + 
  geom_col() + 
  facet_wrap(~CourseId, scales = "free_y", nrow = 2) + 
  labs(
    y = "word"
  )
```

## Your turn

::: {.task .nonincremental}
Explore the `en_coursera_reviews` dataset with the folks around you. Can you replicate my analyses, and try a few of your own? Here are some ideas: 

- Explore another subject (besides `data-science`)
- `Label` contains the numeric rating for the course. How does the review text differ between highly rated and low-rated courses?
- `stopwords::stopwords_getsources()` gives the available stopword dictionaries. How do these differ, and how do they change your results?
:::
